title: Introduction to CSS clipping
content:
Introduction to CSS clipping

CSS clipping enables you to define visible portions of an element while hiding other parts, effectively "clipping" its content within a specific shape or area. With clipping, elements aren't limited to being rendered as rectangles and can be designed in visually engaging ways. This guide explores the   property along with some examples.

CSS clipping

Clipping is a CSS technique used to clip (hide) sections of an element, displaying only the area of the element located within a developer-defined path. Clips areas are created by vector paths; anything in the path is visible while areas outside the path are hidden.

The   property

The   property applies clipping. The value it accepts is a vector path defining the area of the element that should remain visible. The path can be defined using boxes, a reference to an  SVG  , or CSS  shapes and paths . In the following example, we clip a blue square  , creating a diamond, using the   function as the clipping path:

css

With the   property, you can make complex shapes by clipping an element to a   or to an  SVG source . You can  animate and transition   shapes  if the declared states have the same number of vector points.

Values of the   property

To visually clip an element, the   property is set to either a  , a   to a   clip source, or a   created with  shape function .

Geometry boxes

The   hides everything outside of the clipped region. The most basic clipping is done via a geometry box. You can clip an element based on it's margin, border, padding, or content. The effects of these visual box values can achieved via other CSS properties, such as setting the   to transparent and the   to the desired visual box. We're looking at these values mostly because these values are using in conjunction with the shape functions, which we'll look at later, to define the origin of the shape clip path.

Understanding the reference box  used by CSS shapes is important when using  , especially with  basic shapes , as the reference box defines a shape's coordinate system.

Visual box values

This live example demonstrates the   property's different visual box values on an element, while comparing it to the CSS   property. We've applied a  , a  , a  , and   to the  . Select a radio button to update the   to a different   value, which updates the   and the   resolved values.

css

When a   box is specified in combination with a  , the value defines the reference box for the basic shape. If specified by itself, it causes the edges of the specified box, including any corner shaping (such as a  ), to be the clipping path.

Shape origin

The previous example may make you think that the   values are useless, as you can use   instead. And you can. But when clipping using basic shapes, the  , when included along with a   as the   value, defines the reference box for, or origin of, that shape. We can combine the two previous examples to demonstrate this.

css

For another example, see   shapes and geometry boxes .

Even values like   can be useful. In addition to creative visuals made by placing the clip-path's edge at the margin-box edge, any computed value for  , other than  , results in the creation of a new  stacking context  the same way that CSS   does for values other than  .

Clipping to basic shapes

The   property's support of   values provides a powerful way to shape elements. The various shape function enable defining precise clipping regions, effectively sculpting elements into unique forms. The basic shape functions, include:

The size and position of these shapes are defined by the   value, which defaults to border-box being used as the reference box if the   value includes a shape without the   component value.

Some of these functions appear to only provide basic predefined clipping options. They may appear to replicate effects you can created with  , but if you  toggled the   property in the previous example, you may have noticed the power of CSS clipping. Shapes provide even more control. For example,   enables clipping a rectangle with precise margins. The real power and control comes with  ,  , and even  , which allows for custom multi-point shapes.

Creating polygons

With  , by defining pairs of coordinates, each of which represents a vertex of the shape. you can create intricate forms like stars or abstract figures. The coordinates define vector points connected by straight lines.

Here we use the   function to create a star:

css

Animation

Clipped shapes can be animated and transitioned by declaring the same number of vector points for the different states.

css

The   function

The   function enables drawing shapes using SVG commands. The function accepts the equivalent of the SVG   attribute as the function's parameter.

The star from the previous example can be created using  :

css

Curved lines

With  , we are not limited to straight lines. In this example, we use the   function to create a heart:

css

SVG as source

Instead of passing an SVG   attribute string as the   function argument, the value of the   property can reference the SVG   element directly.

html

The   of the   is the parameter of the   function.

css

Shape function

The SVG path syntax is not the most intuitive. For this reason, CSS also offers a   function. The   function also takes path drawing directive, but with a syntax that is more human readable. We can recreate the heart with more declarative CSS:

css

The   function is more robust in that it accepts CSS values and units (  is limited to coordinates), including using CSS math functions like  . By using variables, we can create shapes (and boxes) of many different sizes:

css

html

Wrapping text around your clipped shapes

Clipped elements are still rectangular boxed. Clipping means your element doesn't look like a box; but it is still a box. To wrap inline content around the non-rectangular (or rectangular) shapes you define, use the   property. By default, inline content wraps around its margin box;   provides a way to customize this wrapping, making it possible to wrap text around the elements you've clipped, following the clip path you replicated rather than the element's rectangular box.

The content includes two elements to be clipped and the content that will be shaped around them.

html

In addition to applying the same shape for both the   and   properties, the clipped element has to be floated so that the clipped element is on the same line as the content.

css

See also

Overview of shapes

title: Declaring multiple masks
content:
Declaring multiple masks

CSS masking is a technique that enables you to use images as masks to define which sections of an element are fully visible or semi-opaque. The CSS mask selectively reveals or hides parts of the element based on the alpha channel and in some cases the brightness of the colors of the applied mask images.

CSS masks are the opposite of masks worn at masquerade (masked) balls. At a masked ball, a wearer's face is hidden wherever the mask is opaque and visible wherever you can see through the mask. In CSS, the areas where the composited mask layers are fully opaque reveal the element, while transparent areas hide it.

CSS masks are made up of one or more mask layers. In this guide, we discuss the concept of mask layers and how to declare multiple mask layers using the   shorthand property.

Understanding mask layers

You can apply CSS masking to all HTML elements and most SVG elements. A mask can consist of one or more composited mask layers. You define multiple layers using comma-separated values in the   shorthand property or the   property–even a value set to   counts as a layer.

Each mask layer can contain a  mask image , which is positioned relative to the mask's origin box. The image can be sized, repeated, and clipped. If you include more than one mask image, you can define the way the mask layers are composited or combined. (These features are briefly introduced in this guide. For more details and examples, see the  masking properties guide .)

Syntax for multiple mask layers

The   shorthand property accepts a comma-separated list of mask layers. The syntax for each layer can include the following values:

All the components in a mask layer are optional. However, if you omit the   value, it defaults to a transparent black image, which hides the element in that layer completely.

The   shorthand declaration sets values for all the   properties. Any component not declared within a layer will default to its initial value. The   property also resets all the   properties to their initial values. A   declaration that only includes a   value implicitly sets the following:

css

Defining mask layers with

As long as a comma-separated   property declaration includes at least one value other than  , a mask layer is created for every value in the declaration, even for the   values. This behavior applies whether you're using the   property or the   shorthand. These mask images can be gradients, images, or SVG sources. You can define them using a  CSS gradient , a raster image (such as PNGs), or an SVG   element.

css

The  introductory guide to masking  introduces the different types of mask images and their modes.

The   property is analogous to the   property. Just as with the   property, to include multiple mask images, the image values are separated by commas.

css

Each mask image in a multiple-image declaration creates a mask layer. All the examples in this section create one mask layer, except the   declaration, which creates two.

Mask layers and the   keyword

If   is the only value of the   property, no mask layers are created and no masking occurs.

css

Similarly, when using the   shorthand, if no   value is present other than  , no masking occurs. If any of the following are declared, no mask layers are created and nothing is hidden:

css

Otherwise, as long as there is a   declared that isn't set to  , a mask layer is created for every value in the comma-separated list of values, even when the   value is omitted from a value in the comma-separated list or is explicitly set to  . In other words, a layer is created for each valid comma-separated value, unless the entire property resolves to  .

css

The keyword   within a list of mask sources creates a mask layer, albeit a transparent black image layer. Any elements with the class   will have five mask layers:

We can also create the layers using the   shorthand:

css

If a value in the comma-separated list of values is an empty image, fails to download, references a   element that doesn't exist, or otherwise cannot be displayed (or is set to  ), it still counts as a mask image layer, rendering a transparent black mask image that has no visual effect. If all of the values do this, the element will be fully hidden.

No masking occurs if the entire property resolves to  , which makes the element fully visible. On the other hand, if the value includes multiple layers and at least one is not  , the   layers don't reveal any part of the element (or don't make any part of the element visible). In this example, the value doesn't resolve to  ; but because all non-  images are invalid, masking occurs, and the element will be fully hidden.

A computed value other than   creates a  CSS stacking context .

How mask layers affect   properties

The number of mask layers matters when you're also using individual   properties after or with more specificity than a   declaration.

The   properties include:

: Sets the mode of each mask layer to either   or  , or allows it to default to the source's mode by setting the value to  . The default is  .

: Analogous to the   property with syntax that follows the  's   syntax , it sets the initial position of the mask image relative to the mask layer's origin box, defined by the   property. You can specify one, two, or four   values. The default   positions the top-left corner of the mask at the top-left corner of the mask origin box.

: Analogous to the   property, it specifies the  mask positioning area , which is the mask origin box area within which a mask image is positioned. For example, if the   is  , this property defines whether that is relative to the border's outer edge, the padding's outer edge, or the content's outer edge.

: Analogous to the   property, it determines the area of the element affected by a mask. It defines whether the mask painting area is the border, padding, or content box, restricting the painted content of the element to this area. If the mask layer's   source is an SVG   element, the   property has no effect.

: Analogous to the   property, this is used to size the mask layer. Values can be a single keyword ( ,  , or  ), a single length or percentage, or two space-separated values–each of which may be a length, percentage, or  . The default is  .

: Analogous to the   property, this defines how the mask layer image is tiled after it has been sized and positioned.

: Defines how a mask is combined with the mask layers below it. Each mask layer is either added to, subtracted from, included with, or excluded from the previously composited mask layers below it. Similar to  , there is no analogous   property.

Each   value in a comma-separated list of   component properties applies to a separate mask layer. As stated earlier, an element can have multiple mask layers applied — the number of layers is determined by the number of comma-separated values in the   or   properties. Each   value is matched with a mask layer, in order. If the number of values in the   property is greater than the number of mask layers, any excess values are ignored. If the mask component property has fewer values than the number of mask layers, the   values are repeated.

To learn more about these individual properties, see  CSS mask properties .

Order of shorthand component properties

For the most part, the order of the properties is flexible, but there are a few quirks and exceptions.

Ordering rules for   and

The   value, listed in the syntax as  , comes before the   values, listed in the syntax as  .

Both accept   keywords. In addition,   also accepts  . Because of this, the order of these two matters when you want to set   to any value other than  .

If one   value is present along with the   keyword, then the   sets the   value, and   is set to  . In this case, the order doesn't matter.

If only one   value is present and there is no   keyword, both the   and   components are set to that value. As there is only one value, again the ordering doesn't matter.

If two   values are present, the first sets the   component and the second sets the   component. In this case, the order is very important.

Setting the incorrect order for the   and   values may effect the appearance, but will not cause the declaration to fail.

Ordering rules for   and

You may have noticed a slash between   and  , listed in the syntax as   and  . Both properties accept similar values.

In this case, the order is very important. If only one or a pair of   values are present, it will define the position of the image rather than the size. Including both a position and a size in a mask layer without including the slash between the two will invalidate the entire declaration.

css

If a single pair of   values is present, it sets the   property, and the   will be  . If a layer includes both a   and a  , the   property value must come after the   property value and the values must be separated by a forward slash ( ). The slash is required even if the   is set to a value that is not a valid   value.

css

css

To include a   in a mask layer using the   shorthand, you must include a   value with a slash immediately before it.

Warning:

If you include a size in a mask layer but forget the slash after the position, the entire declaration will become invalid.

title: CSS mask properties
content:
CSS mask properties

CSS masking is a technique that enables you to define visible portions of an element by applying a mask, which selectively reveals or hides parts of the element based on the alpha channels, and optionally colors, of the applied mask images.

The  introductory guide to masking  introduces the different types of mask images and their modes. The guide to  declaring multiple masks  discusses the  mask layers  and the   shorthand property, providing a brief introduction to the shorthand's component properties. In this guide, we explore these component properties in greater detail and look at how they interact. We also explain how, in cases where multiple mask images are declared, the  mask layers are composited , or combined.

CSS masks are composed of one or more mask layers, with a mask layer created for every value in the comma-separated list of   or   values, whether the values are images, mask sources, or the keyword  . Every   is  positioned  relative to an  origin  box. The mask images can be  sized ,  repeated , and  clipped , then composited together with previous layers to create the final visual mask on the element.

The   property

The minimum requirement to create a mask is a   property set to a value other than  .

The keyword   within a list of mask sources creates a mask layer. However, if   is the only value of the   property, no masking occurs.

The mask image can be a  CSS gradient , an  imported image  (such as a PNG, SVG, etc.), or an SVG   element.

In this example, we create five mask layers, including an imported image, two gradients, a layer with no image, and an SVG   source as the mask image.

css

Because one of the mask images is specified as  , only four mask images are applied to the   element, while creating five mask layers.

The importance of

The   layer generally has no visual effect (see the   property  for how it impacts the applied mask), but as each value in a comma-separated list of   values applies to a separate mask layer, the   value serves an important purpose even when it doesn't change the composited mask.

This fourth layer in our five-layer structure will match the fourth value of any other comma-separated   property values. As previously mentioned, the number of layers is determined by the number of comma-separated values in the   property value, even if a value is  . Each   value is matched up with the   values, in order. If the number of values in a   property differs from the number of mask layers, any excess values are ignored, or, if the property has fewer values than the number of mask layers, the values are repeated.

If a   property has a single value, this value applies to all the layers. If we have five values, the fourth value applies to the   layer, with the last value being applied to the   source layer. If there are two comma-separated values, the first value will apply to all the odd layers only, including that   source layer. For example, each   property may have a different number of values:

css

In this case, every odd layer will be repeated along the x-axis while every even layer is repeated along the y-axis. The first and fourth layer images will be centered, while the second and fifth will be positioned in the top left corner. The   means the fifth layer's   image will be repeated along the x-axis starting at the top left corner.

Learn more about  mask layers and the   keyword .

The   property

The   property can be used to set the mode of each mask layer to either   or  , or allow it to default to the source's mode by setting the value to  , which is the default. While most   properties have an analogous   property (  is analogous to the   property, for example),   and   have no analogous   property.

Mask types:   and

Each mask is either an   or a   mask.

With   masks, the alpha-transparency of each mask pixel is important. Wherever the mask is opaque, the corresponding parts of the element will be visible. Wherever the mask is transparent, the corresponding parts of the element will be hidden. Wherever the mask is semi-opaque, the element will be equally semi-opaque. The color of the mask doesn't matter, just the alpha-transparency of the colors.

With   masks, both the  brightness of mask's colors  and the alpha channel determine the opaqueness of the masked areas.

Note:

All subsequent examples use the following image as a   on an element to which masks will be applied:

This example demonstrates the difference between   and   masks. The masks are the same, but in the   mask, only the alpha-transparency of the gradient mask colors matter. In the   example, the R, G, B, and A all matter.

Two containers contain images, while the last is empty but is included to display the gradient we will be using as our  .

html

We declare a   with red, transparent, and semi-transparent red diagonal stripes. This gradient is used as our mask and, for the last container, as the background image:

css

We set different values for the   property for each image:

css

In the   case, only the transparency of the gradient's colors matter. Where the gradient is opaque red, the image is opaque. Where the gradient is transparent, the image is hidden. Where the gradient is 50% opaque, the image is 50% opaque. In the   case, the color's brightness matters! See  Alpha transparency versus luminance  to learn about the equation that uses the color's R, G, B, and A channels to determine the opacity of the mask.

The   default value:

The default value of the   property is  . This value sets the   to match the mask's mode type. The   value resolves to   for every mask except masks where the mask source is an SVG   element.

When an SVG   element is used as the mask source, the   value resolves to the value of the   element's   property. If the   element (not the "masked element") doesn't have the CSS   property defined, this property defaults to the value of the SVG   attribute, if present. If that is omitted as well, the   value will resolve to  .

Continuing with the   example, if we don't explicitly set the   property, it will default to   for each layer, as if we had set the following:

css

or, using the   shorthand:

css

The first mask layer,  , references an image. As this isn't a   element within an  , the   resolves to  , with the opaque parts of this image making the corresponding parts of the element visible, while the transparent or semi-transparent parts are invisible or partially visible.

The   is the second mask layer and   is the third. Again, these aren't   elements, so the   value resolves to  . The masking effect of these layers is determined by the  opaqueness of the gradient mask  by default.

The fourth mask layer has   declared, meaning the mask for this layer is transparent black. The   class sets  . Had   instead been a comma-separated list of five different values, the fourth value would have applied to this   layer, allowing the fifth value to apply to the fifth layer.

The fifth mask layer is comprised of an SVG   element that has   as its  . While the default mask mode of the other layers is  , the default  mask type of SVG   elements  is the   value, or, if not set, the   attribute. If that isn't defined either, the value defaults to  . In other words, the masking effect of the   is determined by both the brightness and transparency of the   element's colors.

If we don't declare the   property at all, and allow it to default to   for each mask layer, the result in this   case would resolve to:

css

or, using the   shorthand:

css

The   property

Analogous to the   property, the   property sets the initial position of the mask image relative to the mask layer's origin box, defined by  the   property . The syntax follows the  's   syntax , with the value being one, two, or four   values, defining one to two relative or absolute position offsets.

One-value syntax

If only one keyword value is specified, that value specifies the mask origin edge against which the mask will be placed with the other dimension being  .

If only one   value is specified, this specifies the X coordinate relative to the left edge of the mask origin, with the Y coordinate set to  .

If two keyword values are specified, the order of the value doesn't matter, but the value can not include two vertical or two horizontal axis values;   and   are both invalid.

Two-value syntax

If two values are present, including one keyword and one   value, the order only matters if the keyword is  :

If the keyword is   or  , it defines the X coordinate relative to the left edge, and the value defines the Y coordinate relative to the top edge.

Similarly, a   or   keyword defines the Y coordinate positioning the element against the top or bottom edge, respectively, with the other value defining the X value relative to the left edge of the mask origin box.

When one value is the   keyword and the other is a  , the first value defines the horizontal position and the second value defines the vertical position.

If two values are present and both are   values, the order again matters; the first value defines the horizontal positioning as an offset from the left edge of the mask-positioning area while the second value defines the vertical position as an offset from the top edge of the mask-positioning area.

Four-value syntax

Mask positions can also be relative to corners other than the top left. The four-value syntax enables offsetting the mask from any corner. The value includes two   offsets, each preceded by the origin side for that offset. Whether you declare the horizontal or vertical pair first doesn't matter, but you must declare the origin-side keyword ( ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  , or  ) before the offset   in each pair, and the two origin-sides can't be from the same axis.

In the two   syntax, the origin sides are   and  , in that order. For example,   is the equivalent of  . When offsetting from the top and left, the offset sides aren't required, but the order matters. With the four value syntax, you can use   to offset the mask image from any edge combinations, such as  , and the order of the sides doesn't matter, as the offset edge is defined by the keyword preceding it rather than the declaration order.

Percentage values

When offsetting using percentage values, the mask's dimension is subtracted from the element's dimension, just as is done with  percentage offsets with  .

Positioning repeating mask images

The   property defines the initial position of the mask image. By "initial position", if the  mask repeats , the browser places the first mask image in the position defined by the   property, thus defining the placement of the mask repetitions.

In this example, we set the position of the first image to  , meaning the first mask will be placed at the bottom right edge of the mask origin box. Because mask images repeat by default, the repeating masks will be positioned against the first placed mask's top and left sides.

css

The   defines the position of the first mask image's placement. This demo shows where the first image is placed:

Because the default value for the   property  is  , the images are repeated along the X and Y axes, based on the position of that first mask:

The two-value example defines the top and left offsets of the original mask. The four-value example combines the previous two previous examples, position the first mask using the same offsets as the second image, but from the same edges as demonstrated in the first image.

In the first image, the first star to be placed is the one on the bottom right, with the repeated stars above and to the left. Because of this positioning, the initial star is not clipped, but the top-most and left-most stars are.

If we don't explicitly set the   property, it will default to   for each layer, with the top-left corner of the mask abutting the top-left corner of the mask origin box. Continuing with the   example, it is as if we had set the following:

css

or, expanding on the example using the   shorthand:

css

The   property

When an element has padding, a border, or both, the   property defines which of these box edge values acts as the mask origin box, or the  mask positioning area , within which a mask image is positioned, for that layer. The   property is analogous to the   property, but with a different initial value and SVG-only values.

HTML elements can have masks contained within their content border box, padding box, or content box. For example, if the   is  , is that relative to the border's outer edge, the padding's outer edge, or the content's outer edge?

In the   masking example, the position defined was relative to the border-box (the default behavior), although it is worth noting that the   had no border or padding set, therefore the content-box, padding-box, and border-box origins would all be the same in this case.

In this example, the   places the initial mask in the top left corner of the   element that has a large border and padding, with a green background color to enable seeing the star masking on the padding area.

css

Change the value of the   property by changing the selected radio button, looking at the position of the top left star mask as you do so.

The default value is  . With this value, the initial mask is placed at the border's top-left edge and is not clipped. When the initial mask is placed at the outer or inner edge of the padding, there is room above it and to the left; these repeating masks are clipped.

Continuing with the   example, if we don't explicitly set the   property, it will default to   for each layer, as if we had set the following:

css

or, expanding on the example using the   shorthand:

css

For SVG elements, which don't have the associated CSS layout boxes, a mask can be contained inside the SVG element's fill, stroke, or view box.

The   property

The   property determines the area of the element that will be affected by a mask, effectively clipping the element at the defined box edge. It is analogous to the   property, but with some different values.

Because the   property accepts all the   values and both have the same   default value, the two properties may seem similar, but they serve very different purposes. While   determines where a mask image will be positioned, the   property causes the original element to have its content clipped to the specified box. It is important to understand them both: if the   causes the   to place the mask image outside the clipping area, the mask will be clipped.

The   property accepts all the   values, as well as its own   value. The   value sets the painted content not to be clipped. You can still cause the mask image to be clipped by positioning it outside of the border content area using   values that are less than zero or resolve to greater than 100%.

Setting the   and   to different values can cause the mask layer image to be clipped. For example, if an element with a border and padding has   set to   and   set to  , and the   is set to the   edge, the mask layer image will be clipped at the top-left edge.

The next example adds clipping options to the previous example to demonstrate the different non-SVG   values and to show how they impact the different   values.

css

The first mask is placed at the top-left edge of the mask origin container, then repeated. If the origin box is the   and the clipping region is the  , the top and left areas of the mask origin container will be clipped. Generally, you will want the   to be the same as the  .

Continuing with the   example, if we don't explicitly set the   property, it will default to   for each layer, as if we had set the following:

css

or, expanding on the example using the   shorthand:

css

In the   shorthand, if only one   value is given, it sets both the   and   property values. If two   values are present, the first defines the   and the second defines the  .

For mask layer images that do not reference an SVG   element, the   property defines whether the mask painting area, or the area affected by the mask, is the border, padding, or content box. The painted content of the element will be restricted to this area.

When the mask layer's   source is a  , the   property has no effect. Rather, the   element's  ,  ,  ,  , and   attributes determine the mask painting area.

The   property

The   property is used to size mask layers. This property is analogous to the   property, taking the same values. When sizing your masks, remember that areas of the element not covered by the mask images are hidden.

There are three ways to declare a  :

the   or   keyword,

one length, percentage, or the keyword  , or

two values that are a combination of lengths, percentages, and the keyword  .

The mask image can be left to its natural size, stretched, or constrained to fit the available space. The  aspect ratio  of the mask image is maintained by default, but declaring two   values can distort the mask image if the ratio of the two values is not the same as the original image (  is the other property/value pair that may distort the mask image).

If the   is set to  , the mask image will be the largest size it can be while being fully contained within the mask positioning area. In this case, the mask image is not clipped, but rather, it is fully contained.

When set to  , the mask image will be the smallest size it can be to fully cover the entire mask-positioning area, with the mask being clipped if the mask's aspect ratio differs from the aspect ratio of the mask-positioning area.

In other words, With   and  , at least one dimension of the mask will be the same size as the mask-positioning area's same dimension; the mask image either grows or shrinks so that either the width is the same width as the mask positioning area or the mask image's height is equal to the mask positioning area's height.

With  ,  , and   values, the size is relative to the origin box. In our star-mask and flag image example, the aspect ratio of both the mask image and the   are  , meaning in this case  ,  , and   will all produce the same sized mask. This example demonstrates how, when   is set to  ,   or a   value, the actual size of the mask may differ depending on the value of the   property :

css

Change the value of the   property to see how the different values impact the mask size:

This example included one   value. When one   value is specified, it defines only the mask width, with the height defaulting to  , which maintains the aspect ratio. When two values are specified, the first defines the mask width and the second defines its height.

The default value of   is  , rendering the mask at its  intrinsic size , the size at which the mask would be displayed if no CSS were applied. The underlying  aspect ratio  of the mask image is maintained if you set a single   value, or two values in the same ratio as the aspect ratio. If you declare two values not in the same ratio as the aspect ratio, the mask image is distorted.

Like with all longhand components of shorthand property, if the   shorthand property is set and the value of the   property is not defined within any mask layer, the   value is reset to its initial value of   for those mask layers.

If the image has no intrinsic proportion, for example in the case of a  CSS gradient , the default   is the entirety of the mask positioning area as set by  the   property .

Continuing with the   example, if we don't explicitly set the   property, it will default to   for each layer, as if we had set the following:

css

or, expanding on the example using the   shorthand, with the   component going after the   value, separated by a forward slash (/):

css

The   property

The   property defines how mask images are repeated, or tiled, after the initial mask image has been sized and positioned. The   property defines if and how that mask image is repeated along the horizontal and vertical axes. In most of the previous examples, you may have noticed the star mask repeated along the X and Y axes. This is because   is the default value.

The   property is analogous to the   property, accepting the same   values. As is the case with  , the first (and possibly only) mask-image repetition is positioned by  the   property  and sized by  the   property . The positions of the repeated background or mask images are based on this initial image instance.

Continuing with the   example, if we don't explicitly set the   property, it will default to   for each layer, as if we had set the following:

css

or, expanding on the example using the   shorthand:

css

The   property

The   shorthand includes the   property, which defines how multiple masks are combined to create the final mask effect. Each value in the comma-separated list of values determines whether the browser should  ,  ,  , or   the associated mask layer from or to the mask layers below it. Similar to  , and the other   properties, there is no property in the   shorthand that is analogous.

In this example, we include two   values, including the star and the gradient from the previous examples as mask images:

css

We set a different   value for each image:

css

The semi-transparent star mask is added to, subtracted from, intersected with, or excluded from the striped mask, depending on the   value.

The   property is only relevant in cases with two or more mask layers. This reads "mask layers", not "mask images" because, if   is included, the transparent black mask is composited. A   value can have a profound effect on masking in the case of   and  . For example, if the   resolves to  , subtracting a black mask will remove the entire mask (the element will be hidden). Similarly, if   is the last layer with   set for that layer, the entire element will be hidden. Here, we add a third layer, with  , to the previous example:

css

Note how the   example excludes everything because the transparent black mask doesn't intersect anything.

If we reverse the order of the mask layers, we can also get very different results:

css

In the first example, the stars are subtracted from the stripes. In the second, the stripes are subtracted from the stars.

Like all the other   component properties,   takes a comma-separated list of values. Because the property effects how masks are combined, this property is only relevant for multiple mask layers and the number of used values is one less than the number of mask layers.

The last pair of masks is composited first. The previous mask image is then composited with the previous composition.

Continuing with the   example, if we don't explicitly set the   property, it will default to   for each layer, as if we had set the following:

css

In this case, the   element will be composited with the   layer. Then the radial gradient will be composited with the result of the previous composition, and so on.

Like we saw with all the other component properties, we could have used the   shorthand:

css

title: Using media queries
content:
Using media queries

Media queries  allow you to apply CSS styles depending on a device's media type (such as print vs. screen) or other features or characteristics such as screen resolution or orientation,  aspect ratio , browser  viewport  width or height, user preferences such as preferring reduced motion, data usage, or transparency.

Media queries are used for the following:

To conditionally apply styles with the  CSS   ,   and    at-rules .

To target specific media for the  ,  ,  , and other  HTML  elements with the   or   attributes.

To  test and monitor media states  using the   and   methods.

Note:

The examples on this page use CSS's   for illustrative purposes, but the basic syntax remains the same for all types of media queries.

Syntax

A media query is composed of an optional  media type  and any number of  media feature  expressions, which may optionally be combined in various ways using  logical operators .

Media queries are case-insensitive.

Media types  define the broad category of device for which the media query applies:  ,  ,  .

The type is optional (assumed to be  ) except when using the   logical operator.

Media features  describe a specific characteristic of the  user agent , output device, or environment:

Deprecated

Deprecated

Deprecated

For example, the   feature allows a query to check whether the device supports hovering over elements.

Media feature expressions test for their presence or value, and are entirely optional.

Each media feature expression must be surrounded by parentheses.

Logical operators  can be used to compose a complex media query:  ,  , and  .

You can also combine multiple media queries into a single rule by separating them with commas.

A media query computes to   when the media type (if specified) matches the device on which a document is being displayed  and  all media feature expressions compute as true.

Queries involving unknown media types are always false.

Note:

A style sheet with a media query attached to its   tag  will still download  even if the query returns  , the download will happen but the priority of downloading will be much lower.

Nevertheless, its contents will not apply unless and until the result of the query changes to  .

You can read why this happens in Tomayac's blog  Why Browser Download Stylesheet with Non-Matching Media Queries .

Targeting media types

Media types describe the general category of a given device.

Although websites are commonly designed with screens in mind, you may want to create styles that target special devices such as printers or audio-based screen readers.

For example, this CSS targets printers:

css

You can also target multiple devices.

For instance, this   rule uses two media queries to target both screen and print devices:

css

See  media types  for the list of available media types.

Because media types describe devices in very broad terms, most of the originally-defined media types were deprecated, with just  ,  , and   remaining. To target more specific attributes, use  media features  instead.

Targeting media features

Media features describe the specific characteristics of a given  user agent , output device, or environment.

For instance, you can apply specific styles to widescreen monitors, computers that use mice, or devices that are being used in low-light conditions.

This example applies styles when the user's  primary  input mechanism (such as a mouse) can hover over elements:

css

Media features are either range or discrete.

Discrete features  take their value from an  enumerated  set of possible keyword values. For example, the discrete   feature accepts either   or  .

css

Many  range features  can be prefixed with "min-" or "max-" to express "minimum condition" or "maximum condition" constraints.

For example, this CSS will apply styles only if your browser's  viewport  width is equal to or narrower than 1250px:

css

The following media queries are equivalent to the above example:

css

With media query range features, you can either use the inclusive   and   prefixes or the more concise range syntax operators   and  .

The following media queries are equivalent:

css

The range comparisons above are inclusive. To exclude the comparison value, use   and/or  .

css

If you create a media feature query without specifying a value, the nested styles will be used as long as the feature's value is not   or  .

For example, this CSS will apply to any device with a color screen:

css

If a feature doesn't apply to the device on which the browser is running, expressions involving that media feature are always false.

For more  Media feature  examples, please see the reference page for each specific feature.

Creating complex media queries

Sometimes you may want to create a media query that depends on multiple conditions. This is where the  logical operators  come in:  ,  , and  .

Furthermore, you can combine multiple media queries into a comma-separated list; this allows you to apply the same styles in different situations, with the contained media queries evaluated as a logical   composition: interpreted as if each media query were within parentheses with an   between them.

In the previous example, we saw the   operator used to group a media  type  with a media  feature .

The   operator can also combine multiple media features within a single media query.

The   operator negates a media query, or a media feature when used with brackets, basically reversing their normal meanings.

The   operator can, under certain conditions, be used to combine multiple media features within a single media query.

Lastly, the   operator was used to prevent older browsers from applying the styles without evaluating the media feature expressions but it has no effect in modern browsers.

Note:

In most cases, the   media type is used by default when no other type is specified.

However, if you use the   operator, you must explicitly specify a media type. You can see   or   as a whole.

Combining multiple types or features

The   keyword combines a media feature with a media type  or  other media features.

This example combines two media features to restrict styles to landscape-oriented devices with a width of at least 30 ems:

css

To limit the styles to devices with a screen, you can chain the media features to the   media type:

css

Testing for multiple queries

You can use a comma-separated list of media queries to apply styles when the user's device matches any one of various media types, features, or states.

The following rule contains two media queries. The block's styles will apply if either the user's device has a height of 680px or more  or  if the browser viewport is in portrait mode (the viewport height is greater than the viewport width):

css

In this example, if the user is printing to a PDF and the page height is 800px, the media query returns true because the first query component — which tests whether the viewport has a height of   or more — is true.

Likewise, if a user is on a smartphone in portrait mode with a viewport height of 480px, the media query returns true because the second query component is true.

In a comma-separated list of media queries, the individual media queries end at the comma or, in the case of the last media query in the list, at the opening bracket ( ).

Inverting a query's meaning

The   keyword inverts the meaning of a single media query. For example, the CSS styles in this media query will apply to everything  except  printed media:

css

The   negates only the media query it is applied to. The  , without parenthesis, negates all the features within the media query in which it is contained. This means, in a comma-separated list of media queries, each   applies to the single query it is contained within, applying to  all  the features within that single query. In this example, the   applies to the first media query  , which concludes at the first comma:

css

Because the query starts with a media type  , you  cannot  wrap   with parentheses. On the other hand, if your media query consists of features only, then you  must  parenthesize the query:

css

Parentheses limit the components of the query that get negated. For example, to negate the   query only:

css

only negates the query to its right. In this example, we negate the   media feature but not the   media type:

css

The   matches if the device has no hover capability. In this case, because of its ordering, the   applies to   but not to  .

Improving compatibility with older browsers

The   keyword prevents older browsers that do not support media queries with media features from applying the given styles.

It has no effect on modern browsers.

css

Testing for multiple features with

You can use   to test for a match among more than one feature, resolving to   if any of the features are true.

For example, the following query tests for devices that have a monochrome display or hover capability:

css

Note that you cannot use the   operator on the same level as the   and   operators. You can either separate the media features with a comma or use parenthesis to group sub-expressions of media features to clarify the order of evaluation.

For example, the following queries are both valid:

css

See also

Container queries

Testing media queries programmatically

CSS Animations Between Media Queries

title: Using media queries for accessibility
content:
Using media queries for accessibility

CSS media queries  can be used to help users with disabilities better experience your website.

Reduced Motion

Blinking and flashing animation can be problematic for people with cognitive concerns such as Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD). Additionally, certain kinds of motion can be a trigger for Vestibular disorders, epilepsy, and migraine and Scotopic sensitivity. Reducing animations or switching animation off completely based on the user's preference can also benefit users with low battery or low-end devices.

The   media query enables providing an experience with fewer animations and transitions to users who have set their operating system's accessibility preferences to reduce motion. It has two possible values:

Indicates that the user has made no preference known to the system.

Indicates that user has notified the system that they prefer an interface that minimizes the amount of movement or animation, preferably to the point where all non-essential movement is removed.

Example

This example has an annoying animation unless you turn on Reduce Motion in  your accessibility preferences .

HTML

html

CSS

css

The value of   is  , not "none". This preference does not mean all animations must be removed, which could be achieved with  . Rather, users expect motion animation, including those triggered by user interaction, to be disabled unless the animation is essential to the functionality or the information being conveyed (see  WCAG: Animation from Interactions ).

title: Testing media queries programmatically
content:
Testing media queries programmatically

Baseline

Widely available

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since July 2015.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The  DOM  provides features that can test the results of a  media query  programmatically, via the   interface and its methods and properties. Once you've created a   object, you can check the result of the  query  or receive notifications when the result changes.

Creating a media query list

Before you can evaluate the results of a media query, you need to create the   object representing the query. To do this, use the   method.

For example, to set up a query list that determines if the device is in landscape or portrait  orientation :

js

Checking the result of a query

Once you've created your media query list, you can check the result of the query by looking at the value of its   property:

js

Receiving query notifications

If you need to be aware of changes to the evaluated result of the query on an ongoing basis, it's more efficient to register a  listener  than to poll the query's result. To do this, call the   method on the   object, with a callback function to invoke when the media query status changes (e.g., the media query test goes from   to  ):

js

This code creates the orientation-testing media query list and adds an event listener to it. After defining the listener, we also call the listener directly. This makes our listener perform adjustments based on the current device orientation; otherwise, our code might assume the device is in portrait mode at startup, even if it's actually in landscape mode.

The   function would look at the result of the query and handle whatever we need to do on an orientation change:

js

Above, we define the parameter as   — an event object of type   that also includes the   and   properties, so you can query these features of the   by directly accessing it, or accessing the event object.

Ending query notifications

To stop receiving notifications about changes to the value of your media query, call   on the  , passing it the name of the previously-defined callback function:

js

Specifications

Specification

CSSOM View Module

#  the-mediaquerylist-interface

Browser compatibility

title: Printing
content:
Printing

There may be times in which your website or application would like to improve the user's experience when printing content. There are several possible scenarios:

You wish to adjust layout to take advantage of the size and shape of the paper.

You wish to use different styles to enhance the appearance of your content on paper.

You wish to use higher resolution images for a better result.

You want to adjust the user experience of printing, such as presenting a specially-formatted version of your content before printing begins.

There may be other cases in which you want to manage the printing process, but these are some of the most common scenarios. This article provides tips and techniques for helping your web content print better.

Using a print style sheet

Add the following to your   tag.

html

Using media queries and @page to control printed content

You can use the CSS   at-rule to set different styles for your webpage when it is printed on paper or as a PDF versus when it is displayed on the screen. The    media type  sets the styles for printed media; these styles will only be used for printed content.

Add this at the end of your stylesheet. Note that  specificity  and precedence rules still apply:

css

You can also use the   at-rule to modify different aspects of printed pages including the page's dimensions, orientation, and margins. The   at-rule can be used to target all pages in a print-out or just a specific subset of pages

Detecting print requests

Browsers send   and   events to determine when printing may have occurred. You can use this to adjust the user interface presented during printing (for example displaying or hiding user interface elements during the print process).

Examples

Here are some common examples.

Automatically close the window when finished

The following example will close the window after the user has printed its contents:

js

Print an external page without opening it

If you want to be able to print an external page without opening it, you can utilize a hidden   (see:  HTMLIFrameElement ), automatically removing it after the user prints its contents. The following is a possible example which will print a file named  :

HTML

html

JavaScript

js

See also

event

event

Media queries

CSS paged media  module

title: CSS nesting at-rules
content:
CSS nesting at-rules

Any  at-rule  whose body contains style rules can be nested inside another style rule using CSS nesting. Style rules nested inside at-rules take their nesting selector definition from the nearest ancestor style rule. Properties can be directly included inside a nested at-rule, acting as if they were nested in a   block.

at-rules that can be nested

Examples

Nesting   at-rule

In this example we see three blocks of CSS. The first one shows how to write typical at-rule nesting, the second is an expanded way of writing the nesting as the browser parses it, and the third one shows the non-nested equivalent.

Nested CSS

css

Expanded nested CSS

css

Non-nested equivalent

css

Multiple nested   at-rules

At-rules can be nested within other at-rules. Below you can see an example of this, and how it would be written without nesting.

Nested at-rules

css

Non-nested equivalent

css

Nesting Cascade Layers ( )

Cascade Layers  can be nested to  create child-layers . These are joined with a   (dot).

Defining the parent & child layers

We start by defining the named cascade layers, prior to using them, without any style assignments.

css

Assigning rules to layers with nesting

Here the   selector assigns its rules to the  base   . The nested  support    creates the   sub-layer, and the   nesting selector is used to create the rules for the   selector.

css

Equivalent without nesting

css

title: CSS nesting and specificity
content:
CSS nesting and specificity

The  specificity  of the   nesting selector is calculated using the largest specificity in the associated selector list. This is identical to how specificity is calculated when using the   function.

html

nesting syntax

css

syntax

css

In this example, the id selector ( ) has a specificity of  , while the type selector ( ) has a specificity of  . The   nesting selector  and   pseudo-class both take a specificity of  , even though the   id selector is never used.

The   class selector has a specificity of  . This makes the total specificity   for   and   for  , meaning that   wins out.

title: Using CSS nesting
content:
Using CSS nesting

The  CSS nesting  module allows you to write your stylesheets so that they are easier to read, more modular, and more maintainable. As you are not constantly repeating selectors, the file size can also be reduced.

CSS nesting is different from CSS preprocessors such as  Sass  in that it is parsed by the browser rather than being pre-compiled by a CSS preprocessor. Also, in CSS nesting, the  specificity of the   nesting selector  is similar to the   function; it is calculated using the highest specificity in the associated selector list.

This guide shows different ways to arrange nesting in CSS.

Child selectors

You can use CSS nesting to create child selectors of a parent, which in turn can be used to target child elements of specific parents. This can be done with or without the   nesting selector .

There are certain instances where using the   nesting selector can be necessary or helpful:

When joining selectors together, such as using  compound selectors  or  pseudo-classes .

For backwards compatibility.

As a visual indicator to aid with readability, when seeing the   nesting selector you know that CSS nesting is being used.

css

Examples

In these examples, one without and one with the   nesting selector, the   inside the   is being styled differently to the   that is a sibling of a  .

Without nesting selector

HTML

html

CSS

css

Result

With nesting selector

CSS

css

Result

Combinators

CSS Combinators  can also be used with or without the   nesting selector.

Example

Nesting the sibling combinator

In this example, the first paragraph after each   is targeted with the  next-sibling combinator ( )  using CSS nesting.

HTML

html

CSS

css

Result

Compound selectors

When using  compound selectors  in nested CSS you  have  to use the   nesting selector. This is because the browser will automatically add whitespace between selectors that do not use the   nesting selector.

In order to target an element with   the   nesting selector is needed otherwise the whitespace will break the compound selector.

css

Example

Nesting and compound selectors

In this example the   nesting selector is used to create compound selectors to style elements with multiple classes.

HTML

html

CSS

Styles for the   to create a column using  flexbox layout .

css

In the CSS code below, nesting is used to create compound selectors both with and without  . The top-level selector defines the basic styles for elements with  . The   nesting selector is then used to create compound selectors for elements with either   or  . Additionally, the use of nesting to create compound selectors without explicitly using   can be seen in the selector  .

css

Result

Appended nesting selector

The   nesting selector can also be appended to a nested selector which has the effect of reversing the context.

This can be useful when we have styles for a child element that change when a parent element is given a different class:

html

As opposed to:

html

css

Example

Appending nesting selector

In this example there are 3 cards, one of which is featured. The cards are all exactly the same except the featured card will have an alternative color for the heading. By appending the   nesting selector the style for the   can be nested in the style for the  .

HTML

html

CSS

css

In the following CSS, we are creating the styles for   and  . Then, in the   style block, we nest the   class with the   nesting selector appended which creates a style for  , which is equivalent to  .

css

Result

Nested declarations rule

The nested declaration rule is that CSS rules are parsed in the order that they are written in the CSS document.

With the following CSS:

css

The   is parsed first and set to silver, then the   rule is evaluated, and finally the  .

The CSSOM parses the CSS in the following way:

Note that in order to preserve the parsing order, all the rules before nesting are handled as top-level  , while any top level rules after nesting are represented as  .

That's why the   is inside a nested declaration even though it is a top level declaration in the original document.

Note:

Support for the rule was added with  .

Browsers that  do not support this interface  may parse nested rules in the wrong order.

Concatenation (is not possible)

In CSS preprocessors such as  Sass , it is possible to use nesting to join strings to create new classes. This is common in CSS methodologies such as  BEM .

css

Warning:

This is not possible in CSS nesting: when a  combinator  is not used, the nested selector is treated as a  type selector . Allowing concatenation would break this.

In  compound selectors , the type selector must come first. Writing   (a  type selector ) makes the CSS selector, and the entire selector block, invalid. As the type selector must come first, the compound selector must be written as  .

css

Invalid nested style rules

If a nested CSS rule is invalid then all of the enclosed styles will be ignored. This does not affect the parent or preceding rules.

In the following example, there is an invalid selector (  is not a valid character for selectors). The rule that includes this selector is ignored, but subsequent valid rules are not.

css
