title: -webkit-mask-repeat-x
content:
-webkit-mask-repeat-x

Non-standard:  This feature is not standardized. We do not recommend using non-standard features in production, as they have limited browser support, and may change or be removed. However, they can be a suitable alternative in specific cases where no standard option exists.

The   property specifies whether and how a mask image is repeated (tiled) horizontally.

Syntax

css

Values

repeat

The mask image is repeated both horizontally and vertically.

no-repeat

The mask image is not repeated; only one copy of the mask image is drawn. The remainder of the masked element's content is not displayed.

repeat

The mask image is repeated both horizontally and vertically.

space

The image is repeated as much as possible without clipping. The first and last images are pinned to either side of the element, and whitespace is distributed evenly between the images. The   property is ignored unless only one image can be displayed without clipping. The only case where clipping happens using space is when there isn't enough room to display one image.

round

As the allowed space increases in size, the repeated images will stretch (leaving no gaps) until there is room for another one to be added. When the next image is added, all of the current ones compress to allow room. Example: An image with an original width of 260px, repeated three times, might stretch until each repetition is 300px wide, and then another image will be added. They will then compress to 225px.

Formal definition

Initial value Applies to all elements Inherited no Computed value as specified Animation type discrete

Formal syntax

Examples

Using a repeating or non-repeating mask image

css

Using multiple mask images

You can specify a different   for each mask image, separated by commas:

css

Each image is matched with the corresponding repeat style, from first specified to last.

Specifications

Not part of any standard.

title: -webkit-mask-repeat-y
content:
-webkit-mask-repeat-y

Non-standard:  This feature is not standardized. We do not recommend using non-standard features in production, as they have limited browser support, and may change or be removed. However, they can be a suitable alternative in specific cases where no standard option exists.

The   property sets whether and how a mask image is repeated (tiled) vertically.

Syntax

css

Values

repeat

The mask image is repeated vertically.

no-repeat

The mask image is not repeated vertically; only one copy of the mask image is drawn in vertical direction. The vertical remainder of the masked element's content is not displayed.

repeat

The mask image is repeated vertically.

space

The image is repeated as much as possible without clipping. The first and last images are pinned to the top and bottom edge of the element, and whitespace is distributed evenly between the images. The   property is ignored unless only one image can be displayed without clipping. The only case where clipping happens using space is when there isn't enough room to display one image.

round

As the allowed vertical space increases in size, the repeated images will stretch (leaving no gaps) until there is room for another one to be added. When the next image is added, all of the current ones compress to allow room. Example: An image with an original height of 260px, repeated three times, might stretch until each repetition is 300px high, and then another image will be added. They will then compress to a height of 225px.

Formal definition

Initial value Applies to all elements Inherited no Computed value for   the absolute value, otherwise a percentage Animation type discrete

Formal syntax

Examples

Using a repeating or non-repeating mask image

css

Using multiple mask images

You can specify a different   for each mask image, separated by commas:

css

Each image is matched with the corresponding repeat style, from first specified to last.

Specifications

Not part of any standard.

The  CSS    property sets the distribution of space between and around content items along a  flexbox 's  cross axis , or a  grid  or  block-level  element's block axis.

This property has no effect on single line flex containers (i.e., ones with  ).

The interactive example below uses grid layout to demonstrate some of the values of this property.

Try it

Syntax

css

Values

The items are packed in their default position as if no   value was set.

The items are packed flush to each other against the start edge of the alignment container in the cross axis.

The items are packed flush to each other in the center of the alignment container along the cross axis.

The items are packed flush to each other against the end edge of the alignment container in the cross axis.

The items are packed flush to each other against the edge of the alignment container depending on the flex container's cross-start side.

This only applies to flex layout items. For items that are not children of a flex container, this value is treated like  .

The items are packed flush to each other against the edge of the alignment container depending on the flex container's cross-end side.

This only applies to flex layout items. For items that are not children of a flex container, this value is treated like  .

,  ,

Specifies participation in first- or last-baseline alignment: aligns the alignment baseline of the box's first or last baseline set with the corresponding baseline in the shared first or last baseline set of all the boxes in its baseline-sharing group.

The fallback alignment for   is  , the one for   is  .

The items are evenly distributed within the alignment container along the cross axis. The spacing between each pair of adjacent items is the same. The first item is flush with the start edge of the alignment container in the cross axis, and the last item is flush with the end edge of the alignment container in the cross axis.

The items are evenly distributed within the alignment container along the cross axis. The spacing between each pair of adjacent items is the same. The empty space before the first and after the last item equals half of the space between each pair of adjacent items.

The items are evenly distributed within the alignment container along the cross axis. The spacing between each pair of adjacent items, the start edge and the first item, and the end edge and the last item, are all exactly the same.

If the combined size of the items along the cross axis is less than the size of the alignment container, any  -sized items have their size increased equally (not proportionally), while still respecting the constraints imposed by  /  (or equivalent functionality), so that the combined size exactly fills the alignment container along the cross axis.

Used alongside an alignment keyword. If the chosen keyword means that the item overflows the alignment container causing data loss, the item is instead aligned as if the alignment mode were  .

Used alongside an alignment keyword. Regardless of the relative sizes of the item and alignment container and whether overflow which causes data loss might happen, the given alignment value is honored.

Note:

The   values ( ,  ,  , and  ) have no effect in  block layout  as all the content in that block is treated as a single  alignment-subject .

The  CSS    property sets the   value on all direct children as a group. In flexbox, it controls the alignment of items on the  cross axis . In grid layout, it controls the alignment of items on the block axis within their  grid areas .

The interactive example below uses grid layout to demonstrate some of the values of this property.

Try it

Syntax

css

Values

The effect of this keyword is dependent of the layout mode we are in:

In absolutely-positioned layouts, the keyword behaves like   on  replaced  absolutely-positioned boxes, and as   on  all other  absolutely-positioned boxes.

In static position of absolutely-positioned layouts, the keyword behaves as  .

For flex items, the keyword behaves as  .

For grid items, this keyword leads to a behavior similar to the one of  , except for boxes with an  aspect ratio  or an intrinsic size where it behaves like  .

The property doesn't apply to block-level boxes, and to table cells.

The flex items' margin boxes are centered within the line on the cross-axis. If the cross-size of an item is larger than the flex container, it will overflow equally in both directions.

The items are packed flush to each other toward the start edge of the alignment container in the appropriate axis.

The items are packed flush to each other toward the end edge of the alignment container in the appropriate axis.

The items are packed flush to the edge of the alignment container's start side of the item, in the appropriate axis.

The items are packed flush to the edge of the alignment container's end side of the item, in the appropriate axis.

,  ,

All flex items are aligned such that their  flex container baselines  align. The item with the largest distance between its cross-start margin edge and its baseline is flushed with the cross-start edge of the line.

If the item's cross-size is  , the used size is set to the length necessary to be as close to filling the container as possible, respecting the item's width and height limits. If the item is not auto-sized, this value falls back to  , and to   or   if the container's   is   (or  ) or  .

In the case of  anchor-positioned  elements, aligns the items to the center of the associated anchor element in the block direction. See  Centering on the anchor using  .

Used alongside an alignment keyword. If the chosen keyword means that the item overflows the alignment container causing data loss, the item is instead aligned as if the alignment mode were  .

Used alongside an alignment keyword. Regardless of the relative sizes of the item and alignment container and whether overflow which causes data loss might happen, the given alignment value is honored.

There are also two values that were defined for flexbox, as they are base on  flex model axes  concepts, that work in grid layouts as well:

Used in flex layout only, aligns the flex items flush against the flex container's main-start or cross-start side. When used outside of a flex formatting context, this value behaves as  .

Used in flex layout only, aligns the flex items flush against the flex container's main-end or cross-end side. When used outside of a flex formatting context, this value behaves as  .

Formal definition

They set the   value of the container, and change its background and border colors providing an additional indicator that the layout has changed. The six flex items each have a different background color, with the 4th item being two lines long and the 6th item having an enlarged font.

css

title: alignment-baseline
content:
alignment-baseline

Baseline

2026

*

Newly available

Since March 2026, this feature works across the latest devices and browser versions. This feature might not work in older devices or browsers.

* Some parts of this feature may have varying levels of support.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    CSS  property specifies the specific  baseline  used to align the box's text and inline-level contents.  Baseline alignment  is the relationship among the baselines of multiple alignment subjects within an alignment context. When performing baseline alignment, the   property value specifies which baseline of the box is aligned to the corresponding baseline of its alignment context.

Note:

The   property only has an effect on inline-level boxes, flex items, grid items, table cells, and the  ,  , and   SVG elements. If present, it overrides the shape's   attribute.

In an inline formatting context, inline-level box fragments and glyphs share an alignment context established by their parent inline box fragment along its inline axis. In SVG text layout, these values instead specify the baseline that is aligned to the SVG current text position.

Syntax

css

Values

Use the   value of the parent.

Used in writing Latin, Cyrillic, Greek, and many other scripts; matches the box's alphabetic baseline to that of its parent, corresponding to the bottom of most, but not all characters.

Matches the box's central baseline to the central baseline of its parent, corresponding to the ideographic central baseline, halfway between the ideographic-under and ideographic-over baselines.

Matches the box's ideographic character face under-side baseline to that of its parent, with the derived baseline-table constructed using the ideographic baseline-table in the font.

Matches the box's mathematical baseline to that of its parent, corresponding to the center baseline around which mathematical characters are designed.

Aligns the vertical midpoint of the box with the baseline of the parent box plus half the x-height of the parent. Uses the x-middle baselines; except under   (where the alphabetic and x-height baselines are essentially meaningless), in which case it uses the   baseline instead.

Matches the bottom of the box to the top of the parent's content area, using the line-under edge of an inline's content box.

Matches the top of the box to the top of the parent's content area; the line-over edge of an inline's content box.

Note:

In SVG2, the  ,  , and   were deprecated and   is an alias for  , and   is an alias for  . These keywords should not be used as part of the   shorthand property. Browsers support   as a synonym for   and  , wherein the alignment-point of the object being aligned is aligned with the "hanging" baseline of the parent text content element, but neither is part of the specification.

Formal definition

Initial value Applies to inline-level boxes, flex items, grid items, table cells, and SVG text content elements Inherited no Computed value the specified keyword Animation type discrete

Formal syntax

Example

html

css

Specifications

Specification

CSS Inline Layout Module Level 3

#  alignment-baseline-property

Scalable Vector Graphics (SVG) 2

title: all
content:
all

Baseline

Widely available

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since January 2020.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    shorthand   CSS  property resets all of an element's properties except  ,  , and  CSS Custom Properties . It can set properties to their initial or inherited values, or to the values specified in another cascade layer or stylesheet origin.

Try it

Constituent properties

This property is a shorthand for all CSS properties except for  ,  , and  custom properties .

Syntax

css

The   property is specified as one of the CSS global keyword values. Note that none of these values affect the   and   properties.

Values

Specifies that all the element's properties should be changed to their  initial values .

Specifies that all the element's properties should be changed to their  inherited values .

Specifies that all the element's properties should be changed to their inherited values if they inherit by default, or to their initial values if not.

Specifies behavior that depends on the stylesheet origin to which the declaration belongs:

If the rule belongs to the  author origin , the   value rolls back the  cascade  to the user level, so that the  specified values  are calculated as if no author-level rules were specified for the element. For purposes of  , the author origin includes the Override and Animation origins.

If the rule belongs to the  user origin , the   value rolls back the  cascade  to the user-agent level, so that the  specified values  are calculated as if no author-level or user-level rules were specified for the element.

If the rule belongs to the  user-agent origin , the   value acts like  .

Specifies that all the element's properties should roll back the cascade to a previous  cascade layer , if one exists. If no other cascade layer exists, the element's properties will roll back to the matching rule, if one exists, in the current layer or to a previous  style origin .

Formal definition

Initial value There is no practical initial value for it. Applies to all elements Inherited no Computed value as the specified value applies to each property this is a shorthand for. Animation type as each of the properties of the shorthand (all properties but   and  )

Formal syntax

Examples

In this example, the CSS file contains styling for the   element in addition to some styling for the parent   element. Various outputs in the Results subsection demonstrate how the styling of the   element is affected when different values are applied to the   property inside the   rule.

HTML

html

CSS

css

Results

A. No   property

This is the scenario in which no   property is set inside the   rule. The   element uses the browser's default styling which gives it a margin, together with a specific background and text color as specified in the stylesheet. It also behaves as a  block  element: the text that follows it is beneath it.

B.

C.

D.

When the   value is applied to the   property in the   rule, the   element doesn't use the browser default styling. Because   is a non-inherited property and   and   are inherited properties, the   element is an  inline  element now (initial value), its   is   (initial value), but its   is still   (inherited value), and its   is   (inherited value).

The   element here is styled as a  block  element, with    ,    , and     - all values inherited from the   rule. Baseline

Newly available

Since January 2026, this feature works across the latest devices and browser versions. This feature might not work in older devices or browsers.

Report feedback

The    CSS  property enables defining an element as an  anchor element  by giving it one or more identifying  anchor names . Each name can then be set as the value of a positioned element's   property to associate it with the anchor.

Syntax

css

Values

The default value. Setting   on an element means that it is not defined as an anchor element. If the element was previously defined as an anchor and associated with a positioned element, setting   disassociates the two.

One or more comma-separated arbitrary custom identifiers defining the name or names of the anchor, which can then be referenced in a   property.

Description

To position an element relative to an anchor element, the positioned element requires three features: an association, a position, and a location. The   and   properties provide an explicit association.

The anchor element accepts one or more   anchor names set on it via the   property. When one of those names is then set as the value of the   property of an element that has its   set to   or  , the two elements are associated. The two elements become tethered by setting a location on the associated element relative to the anchor, making it an "anchor-positioned" element.

If multiple anchor elements have the same anchor name set on them, and that name is referenced by the   property value of a positioned element, that positioned element will be associated with the last anchor element with that anchor name in the source order.

Anchor positioning changes the  containing block  of anchor-positioned elements, making its   relative to its anchor rather than to the nearest positioned ancestor element.

To tether and place a positioned element in a specific location relative to an anchor element, an anchor positioning feature is needed, such as the   function (set within an  inset property's  value) or the   property.

You cannot associate a positioned element with an anchor element if the anchor is hidden, such as with   or  , or if the anchor is part of the  skipped contents  of another element due to it having   set on it.

The   property is supported on all elements that generate a principal box. This means that  pseudo-elements , including generated content created using   and  , and UI features like the   input  thumb ( ) can be anchor elements. Pseudo elements are implicitly anchored to the same element as the pseudo-element's originating element, unless otherwise specified.

For more information on anchor features and usage, see the  CSS anchor positioning  module and the  Using CSS anchor positioning  guide.

This example tethers a positioned element to an anchor, positioning the element to the right of the anchor.

We also include some filler text around the two  s to make the   taller so that it will scroll.

CSS

css

We associate the second   with the anchor element by setting its anchor name as the value of the positioned element's   property. We then set the positioned element's:

property to  , converting it to an  anchor-positioned element  so it can be positioned relative to the anchor's position on the page.

and   properties to   functions with values of   and   respectively. This positions the infobox's left edge flush to the right edge of its anchor, and its top edge relative to the top edge of its anchor.

Scroll the page to see how the infobox is positioned relative to the anchor. As the anchor scrolls upwards, the positioned element moves along with it.

Multiple positioned elements

This example demonstrates how you can associate multiple positioned elements with one anchor.

Each of the two positioned elements are associated with the anchor element by setting its anchor name as the positioned element's   property value. Both are also given   positioning, making them  anchor positioned elements . The positioned elements are then positioned in different places relative to the anchor using a combination of inset properties as seen above and   /   properties with a value of  , centrally aligning the infobox to the center of the anchor in the inline/block directions respectively.

title: anchor-scope
content:
anchor-scope

Baseline

2026

Newly available

Since January 2026, this feature works across the latest devices and browser versions. This feature might not work in older devices or browsers.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    CSS  property can be used to limit the scope in which a positioned element can be associated with anchor elements to a particular subtree.

Syntax

css

Values

No anchor scope limiting happens on an element. This is the default value.

Sets the scope so that  any    values set in the subtree can only be bound to by positioned elements in the same subtree.

One or more comma-separated  s representing anchor names. Sets the scope so that the specified   values, when set in the subtree, can only be bound to by positioned elements in the same subtree.

Description

When multiple  anchor elements  on a page are given the same   value and a positioned element is associated with that anchor name (by specifying the name as its   property value), the positioned element will be associated with the  last  anchor element in the source order with that anchor name.

This can be a problem in certain situations. For example, if a document contains multiple repeated components, each with a positioned element tethered to an anchor, all the positioned elements will be anchored to the last anchor on the page unless each component uses a different anchor name. This is likely not the desired behavior.

The   property can fix this problem by limiting the visibility, or "scope", of an   value to a specific subtree. The result is that each positioned element can only be anchored to an element within the same subtree of the element that has the scope set on it.

sets the scope so that  any    values set in the subtree can only be bound to by positioned elements in the same subtree. Let's say we include multiple anchors in a document, all with   set on them, and place them in separate containers. We will then set   on each container. If we then include a positioned element inside one of the containers and give it   as the value of its   property, it will be positioned relative to the anchor inside the same container.

Furthermore, if we create another positioned element outside the containers and give it the same anchor name, or a different anchor name, it will not be anchor-positioned relative to any of the anchors, regardless of whether the anchors have those anchor names included inside their   values.   limits the anchor scope for containers it is set on for  any  anchors, regardless of  , to only positioned elements inside the same containers.

sets the scope so that the specified   values, when set in the subtree, can only be bound to by positioned elements in the same subtree. If we return to the same example described in the previous bullet, but change the   value set on the containers to  :

Positioned elements with   set on them will be limited to the scope imposed by the   setting. They will only be positioned relative to the anchors if they are placed inside the containers.

However, positioned elements with different   names — for example,   —  can  be positioned relative to one of the anchors, whether it is placed inside or outside the containers, provided you add the   anchor name to the anchor's   property. The   property only limits the scope for the   anchor name, so it will have no effect on other anchor names.

If multiple anchors are given the   anchor name, the positioned elements with that   value will be positioned relative to the last anchor in the source order with that name.

is the default value, which specifies that no anchor scope is set. If multiple anchors exist in a document with the same  , and a positioned element is given this name as the value of its   property, it will be positioned relative to the last anchor element in the source order, regardless of where it is placed in the DOM hierarchy.

If you have, for example, three   values set inside a subtree (say,  ,  , and  ), setting   on the top-level element of the subtree would be equivalent to setting  .

Anchor scopes only affect  explicit anchor associations , that is, those made between an anchor element with an   set on it, and a positioned element referencing the anchor element's anchor name in its   value. Anchor scopes do not affect  implicit anchor associations .

For more information on anchor features and usage, see the  CSS anchor positioning  module and the  Using CSS anchor positioning  guide.

Formal definition

Initial value Applies to all elements Inherited no Computed value as specified Animation type discrete

Formal syntax

Examples

Basic usage

This example demonstrates how anchor scope works at a basic level. It shows how an anchor element inside a scoped container can be limited to only having positioned elements inside the same scoped container tethered to it.

HTML

We specify a   element that we will set an anchor scope on. This contains two   elements, one to be turned into an anchor, and one to be an anchor-positioned element.

We also include a third   outside the  , which we will also turn into an anchor-positioned element.

html

CSS

We first set   on the  . This limits its scope so that anchor element descendants of the   with a name of   can only be bound to by positioned elements that are also descendants of the  .

To test this out, we declare the     as an anchor element by setting an   of   on it. We then absolutely position the   elements, tether them to the anchor by setting their   values to  , and position them to the right of the anchor by setting their   values to  :

css

Result

The example renders like so:

Note how the first positioned element is positioned to the right of the anchor. It is in scope for positioning relative to the   anchor, as it is inside the   element where the   is set.

However, the second positioned element is not positioned relative to the anchor. It is not a descendant of the   element, so it is outside the anchor scope.

Comparing different   values

This example demonstrates the effects of the different   values by allowing you to apply different   values to multiple containers, all containing anchors with the same   value.

HTML

We specify three   elements that we will set an anchor scope on. Each   contains two   elements, one to be turned into an anchor, and one to be an anchor-positioned element.

We also include an additional   outside the   elements, which we will also turn into an anchor-positioned element. This will have different anchor positioning settings from the others.

Finally, we include a   containing three different   elements to allow different   values to be set on the   elements.

html

CSS

We start by specifying our anchor elements as anchors by giving them two   values:   and  .

css

Next, we position our   elements relative to an anchor element. We absolutely position them, give them a   value of   to associate them with an anchor, and position them relative to the anchor with a   value of  .

The   element is positioned in a similar way, except that it is given the other available anchor name as its   value —   — and it is positioned to the   of the anchor instead. We also give it a   value of  , so that if the anchor positioning is not working, it will be positioned to the bottom of the  . This element is not contained inside any scoped element, therefore it will only be anchor positioned when certain   values are set on the scoped elements, as explained later on.

css

We handle setting   on the   elements when different radio buttons are pressed using JavaScript, which has been hidden for brevity.

Result

The example renders as follows:

Check out the initial positioning effect applied to the positioned elements with   set on the   elements, and then try selecting the other available   values to see what their effect is. You should observe the following:

: The scope for positioning elements relative to anchor elements that are descendants of the   elements is limited to positioned elements that are themselves descendants of the   elements, regardless of the   value used to associate them. As a result, the positioned elements inside the   elements ("Positioned 1–3") are anchor-positioned as expected, but the positioned element outside the   elements ("Positioned 4") is not anchor-positioned. It is out of scope.

: The scope for positioning elements relative to anchor elements that are descendants of the   elements is limited to positioned elements that are themselves descendants of the   elements, only if the     is used to associate them. As a result, the positioned elements inside the   elements ("Positioned 1–3") are anchor-positioned as expected, and the positioned element outside the   elements ("Positioned 4") is also anchor-positioned as expected. In the former case, the positioned elements are inside the set scope, and in the latter case, the positioned element is not affected by the set scope, as it is using an anchor name outside the scope ( ). The "Positioned 4" element is positioned relative to the last anchor element in the source that has the matching anchor name.

: As no anchor scope is set on the   elements, all of the positioned elements are positioned relative to the last anchor element in the source order.

The    shorthand   CSS  property applies an animation between styles. It is a shorthand for  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  , and  .

Try it

Constituent properties

This property is a shorthand for the following CSS properties:

Syntax

css

Values

One or more single   declarations, separated by commas, with each   including:

or

The name of a   at-rules that specifies the animation to apply to an element. The initial value for   is  .

Determines the length of time that an animation takes to complete one cycle. The value must be one of those available in  . The initial value is  .

Determines the type of transition. The value must be one of those available in  . The initial value is  .

Determines the amount of time to wait from applying the animation to an element before beginning to perform the animation. The value must be one of those available in  . The initial value is  .

The direction in which the animation is played. The value must be one of those available in  . The initial value for   is  .

The number of times the animation is played. The value must be one of those available in  . The initial value for   is  .

Determines how styles should be applied to the animation's target before and after its execution. The value must be one of those available in  . The initial value for   is  .

Determines whether the animation is playing or not. The value must be one of those available in  . The initial value for   is  .

Determines the timeline used to control the progress of the animation. The value must be one of those available in  . The initial value is  .

Description

The   property is specified as one or more single animations, separated by commas. Each   within the comma-separated list of animations sets the  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  , and  . If any of the components are not included in an   declaration, the component value is set to the component's initial value.

animation-name

The   component of each animation is the name for the animation, which may be  , a  , or a  . The initial value of   is  , meaning if no   value is declared in the   shorthand property, no animation is applied to any of the properties.

The order of other values within an animation definition is important for distinguishing an   value from other values. If a value in the   shorthand can be parsed as a value for an animation property other than  , then the value will be applied to that property first and not to  . For this reason, the recommended practice is to specify a value for   as the last value in a list of values when using the   shorthand; this holds true even when you specify multiple, comma-separated animations using the   shorthand.

Time values

Each animation can include zero, one, or two occurrences of the   value. The order of time values within each animation definition is important: the first value that can be parsed as a   is assigned to the  , and the second one is assigned to  .

When no   value is specified in the   shorthand property, the duration defaults to  . In this case, the animation will still occur (the   and   events will be fired), but no animation will be visible to the user.

animation-timeline

The current implementations of   are reset-only: if no   is included in the   shorthand, the shorthand declaration will reset any previously-declared   values to  .

By default, the   is the  . If a value is included, but the user-agent doesn't support   values within the shorthand, the declaration is invalid and ignored.

This means that, when creating  CSS scroll-driven animations , you need to declare the   property after declaring any   shorthand for it to take effect.

Alternatively, the   can be used within the   shorthand within a CSS   block, such as:

css

animation-fill-mode and new stacking contexts

In the case of the    forwards  value, animated properties behave as if included in a set   property value. If a new stacking context is created during the animation, the target element retains the stacking context after the animation has finished.

Accessibility

Blinking and flashing animation can be problematic for people with cognitive concerns such as Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD). Additionally, certain kinds of motion can be a trigger for Vestibular disorders, epilepsy, and migraine and Scotopic sensitivity.

Consider providing a mechanism for pausing or disabling animation as well as using the  Reduced Motion Media Query  to create a complimentary experience for users who have expressed a preference for reduced animated experiences.

An Introduction to the Reduced Motion Media Query | CSS-Tricks

Responsive Design for Motion | WebKit

Animating  CSS Box Model  properties is discouraged. Animating any box model property is inherently CPU intensive; consider animating the  transform  property instead.

Sun Rise

to the center of the viewport and then falls out of sight.

Animating Multiple Properties

Adding onto the sun animation in the previous example, we add a second animation changing the color of the sun as it rises and sets. The sun starts off dark red when it is below the horizon and changes to a bright orange as it reaches the top.

css

Applying Multiple Animations

Here is a sun that rises and falls on a lightblue background. The sun

gradually rotates through a rainbow of colors. The timing of the sun's

position and color are independent.

Cascading Multiple Animations

is 'overwritten' by the bounce animation.

title: animation-composition

animation-composition

Baseline

Widely available

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since July 2023.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    CSS  property specifies the  composite operation  to use when multiple animations affect the same property simultaneously.

Syntax

css

Note:

When you specify multiple comma-separated values on an   property, they will be applied to the animations in the order in which the  s appear. If the number of animations and compositions differ, the values listed in the   property will cycle from the first to the last  , looping until all the animations have an assigned   value. For more information, see  Setting multiple animation property values .

Values

The effect value overrides the underlying value of the property. This is the default value.

The effect value builds on the underlying value of the property. This operation produces an additive effect. For animation types where the addition operation is not commutative, the order of the operands is the underlying value followed by the effect value.

The effect and underlying values are combined. For animation types where the addition operation is not commutative, the order of the operands is the underlying value followed by the effect value.

Description

Each property that is targeted by the  @keyframes  at-rule is associated with an effect stack. The value of the effect stack is calculated by combining the  underlying value  of a property in a CSS style rule with the  effect value  of that property in the keyframe. The   property helps to specify how to combine the underlying value with the effect value.

For example, in the CSS below,   is the underlying value, and   is the effect value. The   property specifies the operation to perform to produce the final effect value after compositing the effect of the underlying value and the effect value.

css

Consider different values for the   property in the above example. The final effect value in each of those cases will be calculated as explained below:

With  ,   will replace   in the   keyframe. This is the default behavior of the property.

With  , the composite effect value in the   keyframe will be  .

With  , the composite effect value in   keyframe will be  .

Note:

A composite operation can also be specified in a keyframe. In that case, the specified composite operation is used for each property first within that keyframe and then on each property in the next keyframe.

With  , the final effect value for the   property in the   keyframe is  . So the element is first moved 100px to the right, rotated 45deg around the origin, then moved to the right by 30px.

title: animation-delay
content:
animation-delay

Baseline

Widely available

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since September 2015.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    CSS  property specifies the amount of time to wait from applying the animation to an element before beginning to perform the animation. The animation can start later, immediately from its beginning, or immediately and partway through the animation.

It is often convenient to use the shorthand property   to set all animation properties at once.

Try it

Syntax

css

Values

The time offset, from the moment at which the animation is applied to the element, at which the animation should begin. This may be specified in either seconds ( ) or milliseconds ( ). The unit is required.

A positive value indicates that the animation should begin after the specified amount of time has elapsed. A value of  , which is the default, indicates that the animation should begin as soon as it's applied.

A negative value causes the animation to begin immediately, but partway through its cycle. For example, if you specify   as the animation delay time, the animation will begin immediately but will start 1 second into the animation sequence. If you specify a negative value for the animation delay, but the starting value is implicit, the starting value is taken from the moment the animation is applied to the element.

Note:

When you specify multiple comma-separated values on an   property, they are applied to the animations in the order in which the  s appear. For situations where the number of animations and   property values do not match, see  Setting multiple animation property values .

Note:

has no effect on  CSS scroll-driven animations .

Formal definition

Initial value Applies to all elements,   and    pseudo-elements Inherited no Computed value as specified Animation type Not animatable

content:
animation-direction

Baseline

Widely available

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since September 2015.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    CSS  property sets whether an animation should play forward, backward, or alternate back and forth between playing the sequence forward and backward.

It is often convenient to use the shorthand property   to set all animation properties at once.

Try it

Syntax

css

Values

The animation plays  forwards  each cycle. In other words, each time the animation cycles, the animation will reset to the beginning state and start over again. This is the default value.

The animation plays  backwards  each cycle. In other words, each time the animation cycles, the animation will reset to the end state and start over again. Animation steps are performed backwards, and easing functions are also reversed. For example, an   easing function becomes  .

The animation reverses direction each cycle, with the first iteration being played  forwards . The count to determine if a cycle is even or odd starts at one.

The animation reverses direction each cycle, with the first iteration being played  backwards . The count to determine if a cycle is even or odd starts at one.

Note:

When you specify multiple comma-separated values on an   property, they are applied to the animations in the order in which the  s appear. For situations where the number of animations and   property values do not match, see  Setting multiple animation property values .

Note:

When creating  CSS scroll-driven animations , specifying an   works as expected, for example   causes the animation to run in reverse over the course of the timeline's progression. A value of   (combined with an  ) causes the animation to run forwards and backwards as the timeline is progressed.

Values

For time-based animations,   is equivalent to a value of   (see below). For  CSS scroll-driven animations ,   fills the entire timeline with the animation.

The time that an animation takes to complete one cycle. This may be specified in either seconds ( ) or milliseconds ( ). The value must be positive or zero and the unit is required.

If no value is provided, the default value of   is used, in which case the animation still executes (the   and   events are fired). Whether or not the animation will be visible when the duration is   will depend on the value of  , as explained below:

If   is set to   or  , the first frame of the animation as defined by   will be displayed during   countdown.

If   is set to   or  , the last frame of the animation will be displayed, as defined by  , after the   expires.

If   is set to  , the animation will have no visible effect.

Note:

Negative values are invalid, causing the declaration to be ignored. Some early, prefixed, implementations may consider them as identical to  .

Note:

When you specify multiple comma-separated values on an   property, they are applied to the animations in the order in which the  s appear. For situations where the number of animations and   property values do not match, see  Setting multiple animation property values .

Note:

When creating  CSS scroll-driven animations , specifying an   value in seconds or milliseconds doesn't really make sense. In tests, it seemed to have no effect on scroll progress timeline animations, while on view progress timeline animations it seemed to push the animation to happen nearer the end of the timeline. However, Firefox requires an   to be set for it to successfully apply the animation. You are therefore advised to set   to   so that animations will work in Firefox, but the effect is not altered too much by it.

It is often convenient to use the shorthand property   to set all animation properties at once.

It is often convenient to use the shorthand property   to set all animation properties at once.

Values

A special keyword denoting no keyframes. It can be used to deactivate an animation without changing the ordering of the other identifiers, or to deactivate animations coming from the cascade.

An unquoted name identifying the animation. This identifier is composed of a combination of case-sensitive letters   to  , numbers   to  , underscores ( ), and/or dashes ( ). The first non-dash character must be a letter. Also, two dashes are forbidden at the beginning of the identifier. Furthermore, the identifier can't be  ,  ,  , or  .

A series of characters following the same rules as custom identifiers, as described above, except that they are surrounded by either double (") or single (') quotes. If using a quoted string for both the   and the corresponding   at-rule name,  , global keywords, and names starting with an underscore or double dashes are valid, though not recommended.

The    CSS   shorthand property  is used to set the start and end of an animation's attachment range along its timeline, i.e., where along the timeline an animation will start and end.

Constituent properties

This property is a shorthand for the following CSS properties:

Syntax

css

The   shorthand property is specified as one or more single animation ranges, separated by commas. Each animation range is specified as one to four space separated values composed of   values,   values, and/or the keyword  .

Values

The keyword  , a  , a  , or a   pair, representing the  . If a   is set without a  , the   defaults to  .

The keyword  , a  , a  , or a   pair, representing the  . If a   is set without a  , the   defaults to  .

Description

The   shorthand property sets the   and   values, defining where along the animation timeline the animation will start and end. By default, the styles defined in a keyframe animation are only applied to an element while that element is being animated. When a keyframe animation is applied to an element depends on the animation timeline of that animation. By default, animation are only applied between the timeline's range start and range end. To apply the animation outside of this range, set the   to  ,  , or  . These three   values apply the first keyframe styles until range start, the last keyframe styles after the animation ends, or both before and after, respectively.

The  scroll port  area known as the view progress visibility range is the area inside which the subject element of a  named view progress timeline  animation is deemed to be visible. By default, this is the full range of the scrollport, but it can be adjusted using the   property.

If two values are specified as components of the   property, they will be interpreted in the order   then  . The value of each component is either the keyword  , a  , or a   optionally followed by a  . These values are space-separated. Normal is equal to   for start and   for end. Setting   with a   for either the start or end range is invalid.

Defining range start and defaulting range end

When you define only the  , either by setting a single  , a single  , or the keyword  , or by specifying a single   followed by a single  , the computed value of the   follows specific rules:

If the value is a single   or the keyword  , this value defines the   and the   is implicitly set to  . For example:

is the equivalent to

is the equivalent to

If the value is a single   (without a   following it), that timeline range name is applied to both the   and   components, and the ranges of   and  , respectively, are implied. For example:

is equivalent to

is equivalent to

If the value is a single   with a single   following it, in that order, the pair defines the  , and the defined   is applied to the   at  . For example:

is equivalent to

is equivalent to

Explicitly defining both range start and range end with two values

If two or more values are included in your   declaration and the values are anything other than a single   followed by a  , both   and   are explicitly set.

If you include two values and the first value is the keyword   or a  , that value defines the  , and the second value defines the  . For example:

is equivalent to

is equivalent to

is equivalent to

is equivalent to

Multiple animations

When specifying ranges for multiple animations, the   shorthand property is specified as one or more single animation ranges, separated by commas. Each animation-range is applied to the animations in the order in which the  s appear. For situations where the number of animations and the   property values do not match, if there are more   value than animations, the extra ranges are ignore. If there are more animations than ranges, the list of   values are repeated until there is a corresponding range for each animation. For example, if we set  , the animation range of all the odd numbered animations   and all the even animations will be  .

Formal definition

Formal syntax

Examples

Basic usage of the   property

In this example, we reduce the duration of the view progress scroll animation by using the   property to offset the start and end of the animation, and demonstrate the effect of the   property on shortened animation timelines.

HTML

In the middle of a wall of text, we include an element that we will animate. We include a lot of text to ensure that our content overflows its container, but this is hidden for the sake of brevity.

We also include a checkbox to toggle the   property on and off, to demonstrate its effect on shortened animation timelines. This is also hidden.

html

CSS

We define an animation that animates an element's opacity, scale, and background color, causing it to fade in, scale up, and change color as the animation progresses. We apply this animation to the   with the   shorthand.

A view progress timeline is created by setting the   function as the value of the   property on our  . The result is that the element animates as it moves upwards through the document as it is scrolled. We declare   property after the shorthand, as the shorthand resets this property.

Lastly, an   declaration is set to make the animation begin later than expected and finish earlier.

css

We also include conditional styling: when the checkbox is checked, the   property gets applied to the animated element:

css

The other styles are hidden for the sake of brevity.

Result

Scroll to see the element being animated.

Note how the  , or  , keyframe property values are not applied to the animated element until the top block border edge is   past the container's bottom edge; it is full size, fully opaque, and magenta. At that point, the animation is applied and it is styled with the values defined by the    keyframe selector . When the   is reached, 25% from the top of the scrollport, it jumps back to its original styling.

Generally, you will want to set   when creating  scroll-driven animations . The jump to the default state occurs because we did not set the   property on the element, which can be used to apply an animation's styles to an element before and after the animation's execution. We initially omitted the property in this example to better enable visualizing the effects of  .

Check the checkbox to apply the   property to the animated element in then re-scroll: the animation styles should now be continuously applied.

The    CSS  property sets the point on the timeline where an animation should end.

Syntax

css

Values

Represents the end of the timeline. This is the default value.

Specifies a length or percentage value measured from the beginning of the timeline.

Specifies a named timeline range within the overall timeline. The range starts at  .

Specifies a length or percentage value measured from the beginning of the specified named timeline range.

Description

The   property specifies the end of the animation's attachment range. Changing the end of the attachment range can potentially shift the end of the animation, that is, the point where keyframes mapped to   progress land when the iteration count is  , and can also reduce the effective duration of the animation.

The property value can be  , a  , or a   with an optional  . If the   value does not include a  , the percentage defaults to  .

The   property is included in the   shorthand as a reset-only value. This means that using the   shorthand resets any previously declared   value to  ; the shorthand cannot be used to set a new   value. When creating  CSS scroll-driven animations , you should declare    after  declaring any   shorthand to avoid resetting the value to  .

The   property, along with the   property, can also be set by using the   shorthand.

Formal definition

Initial value Applies to all elements Inherited no Percentages Relative to the specified named timeline range if specified, otherwise relative to the entire timeline Computed value A list where each item may be 'normal', a length percentage, or a timeline range name and a length percentage Animation type Not animatable

Formal syntax

Examples

Creating a view progress timeline with a range end

In this example, the   is applied to an element animated via a view progress timeline. This makes the animation reach its last keyframe well before the element reaches the end of it's containing viewport.

HTML

In the middle of a long block of text, we've included an element that we'll animate. We've added a lot of text to ensure that the content overflows its container; the extra text is hidden here for brevity.

html

html

CSS

We've defined a view progress timeline by setting a   function as the value of the   property. This is declared  after  the   shorthand to avoid resetting the longhand property value.

We've also set   to make the animation end earlier than expected.

css

When the checkbox is checked, the   property gets applied to the animated element:

css

The other styles applied in this example has been hidden here for brevity.

Result

Scroll to see the element animate. Then toggle the checkbox at the end of the block of text and scroll again. Notice how the element finishes animating when it is 75% of the way through the viewport, and how it returns to its default state at that point when the   property is not applied.

title: animation-timeline
content:
animation-timeline

Limited availability

This feature is not Baseline because it does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    CSS  property specifies the timeline used to control the progress of a CSS animation.

Syntax

css

Values

The   property is specified as one or more comma-separated values, each of which can be one of the following:

The animation is not associated with a timeline, and no animation occurs.

The animation's timeline is the document's default  . This is the default value.

Defines the root element, nearest scroller, or self as an anonymous scroll progress timeline, and optionally the scroll direction of the scroller.

Defines the nearest ancestor scroll container as an anonymous view progress timeline, optionally overriding the default   axis direction and the   start and ending insets.

The name of a scroll-driven or view-progress timeline, as defined by the scroll container's   or   property (or the   or   shorthand property).

Description

The default timeline for a CSS keyframe animation is the time-based  . The   property can be used to set a named or anonymous scroll progress or view progress timeline. Alternatively, it can be used to explicitly set the default time-based document timeline to control the progress of an element's animation or to have no timeline at all, in which case the element does not animate.

The following types of timelines can be set via  :

The default document timeline, which is progressed through by the passing of time since the document was first loaded in the browser. This is the timeline traditionally associated with CSS animations and is selected with a value of  , or by not specifying an   value at all, as this is the default value.

Scroll progress timeline

The animation is progressed through by scrolling a scrollable element, or  scroller , horizontally or vertically. The element that provides the scroll progress timeline can be specified in two ways:

Named scroll progress timeline

The scroller is explicitly named by setting the   property (or the   shorthand property) to a  ; that   name is then set as the value of the   property.

Anonymous scroll progress timeline

The   property of the element to animate is set to the   function. The function's two optional parameters define the scroller providing the scroll progress timeline and the scroll axis to be used.

View progress timeline

A keyframe animation is progressed through based on the change in visibility of an element inside a scroller; this element is known as the  subject . By default, the timeline is at   when the element first becomes visible at one edge of the scroller, and is at   when its end edge exits the scroller's opposite edge. A view progress timeline can be specified in two ways:

Named view progress timeline

The subject is explicitly named by setting the   property (or the   shorthand property) to a  . When you set the   property of the element to animate to that  , the subject's visibility controls the progression of the element's animation. Note that the element to animate does not have to be the same as the subject.

Anonymous view progress timeline

The   property of the element to animate is set to a   function, causing it to be animated based on its visibility within the scrollport of its nearest parent scroller.

No timeline

All animation timelines can be removed by selecting a value of  . When   is set, no animation will occur as there is no timeline to follow.

The   property is included in the   shorthand as a reset-only value. This means that including   resets a previously-declared   value to  . As this component of the shorthand is reset-only, a specific value cannot be set via  . When creating  CSS scroll-driven animations , you need to declare   after declaring any   shorthand for it to take effect.

If you specify multiple comma-separated values, each   value is applied to a single animation in the order in which the   values appear. If the number of values in the   declaration is greater than the number of   values, the excess timeline values are ignored. If there are fewer   values than   values, the   values are repeated, in order, until every   has an associated timeline.

If two or more timelines share the same   name and the same specificity, the one last declared within the cascade will be used. If no timeline is found that matches a name included within the  , the   associated with that value is not associated with a timeline.

Formal definition

Initial value Applies to all elements Inherited no Computed value a list, each item either a case-sensitive CSS identifier or the keywords  ,  Animation type Not animatable

Formal syntax

Examples

Basic usage

This example demonstrates basic usage of the   property, along with the  ,  , and default ( ) values.

HTML

We have an   with three   children. Each   has a unique   and a   child.

html

CSS

We use  flexible box layout  to set the three sections side-by-side. We use  generated content  to display the  . We style all elements the same, applying the     animation that rotates the element by 1 full turn. Using the   shorthand, we declare infinite, 2-second, linearly progressing iterations of the   animation, alternating the direction of each animation.

css

The only difference is the   declaration (or lack thereof in the case of  ) for each  .

css

Because the   property is included in the   shorthand as a reset-only value, the   must come after the   shorthand, or be applied with greater specificity than the   shorthand, to be applied.

Results

Note that declaring a value of   has the same effect as allowing the   to default to that value, and that   removes all timelines from the element, so no animation in the   case.

Setting a named scroll progress timeline

In this example, the animation timeline is set to a horizontal scroll progress timeline.

HTML

Our container includes three stretcher elements which will be wide enough to ensure our container is a scroll container. The middle one contains a shape that we will animate.

html

CSS

We define the container as a flex container, setting a   on the container that is half the width of it's combined flex children. Adding an   value of   sets it to have a horizontal scrollbar.

Our scroll progress timeline, defined using the   and   properties, is named  . This timeline is applied to our   element using  .

css

The CSS below defines a square that rotates in alternate directions according to the timeline provided by the   property, which is set to the   timeline named above. The animation is set two occur twice, in alternating directions, as it passes through the viewport. We added  notched corners  to make the animation effect more apparent.

css

Result

Scroll to see the element being animated.

Setting an anonymous scroll progress timeline

This example expands on the previous one, applying an anonymous scroll progress timeline using the   function.

CSS

We include all the CSS from the previous example, only setting the   property to override the previous example's value. The timeline is provided by the   value, which selects the scrollbar in the inline direction of the nearest ancestor element that has scrollbars. This is the vertical scrollbar of the   element, as the   elements don't have overflowing content and therefore aren't scroll containers.

css

Result

Scroll to see the square element being animated.

Setting a named view progress timeline

In this example, we demonstrate how to create and apply a named view progress timeline. Two elements will be animated, with different elements serving as the scroller.

HTML

Our HTML includes a lot of text in a container within a scroller, which we've hidden for brevity. In the middle of the wall of text, we include two   elements that we will animate based on the visibility of the element itself in the first case and based on the visibility of its parent in the second case:

html

CSS

We create a keyframe animation that changes the opacity and scale of the element, and apply it to both animated elements:

css

The   element is explicitly named as the scroller for itself by setting the   property to a   and also setting that   name as the value of the   property. In the   case, we set the   as the scroller for the animated element:

css

Additional CSS declarations were hidden for brevity.

Result

Scroll the container to see both elements animate.

Note how the visibility of the   element controls its own animation. In this case, the element is at the   keyframe when the top edge enters the viewport, or visible part of the scrollport, and only reaches the   keyframe when the bottom edge exits the viewport.

The   element only becomes visible when that parent is visible, meaning that when it comes into view, it is already about   of the way through the animation. It is only about   through its animation when it exits the top of the viewport.

title: animation-timing-function
content:
animation-timing-function

Baseline

Widely available

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since September 2015.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    CSS  property sets how an animation progresses through the duration of each cycle.

It is often convenient to use the shorthand property   to set all animation properties at once.

Try it

Syntax

css

Values

The easing function that corresponds to a given animation, as determined by  .

The non-step keyword values ( ,  ,  , etc.) each represent cubic Bézier curves with fixed four-point values, while the   function value allows non-predefined values to be specified. The   easing function divides the input time into a specified number of equal-length intervals. Its parameters include a number of steps and a step position.

Equal to  , animates at an even speed.

Equal to  , the default value, increases in velocity towards the middle of the animation, slowing back down at the end.

Equal to  , starts off slowly, with the speed of the transition of the animating property increasing until complete.

Equal to  , starts quickly, slowing down the animation continues.

Equal to  , with the animating properties slowly transitioning, speeding up, and then slowing down again.

An author defined cubic-bezier curve, where the first and third values must be in the range of 0 to 1.

The function interpolates linearly between the provided easing stop points. A stop point is a pair of an output progress and an input percentage. The input percentage is optional and is inferred if not specified. If an input percentage is not provided then the first and last stop points are set to   and   respectively, and the stop points in the middle receive percentage values derived by linearly interpolating between the closest previous and next points that have a percentage value.

Displays an animation iteration along  n  stops along the transition, displaying each stop for equal lengths of time. For example, if  n  is 5, there are 5 steps. Whether the animation holds temporarily at 0%, 20%, 40%, 60% and 80%, on the 20%, 40%, 60%, 80% and 100%, or makes 5 stops between the 0% and 100% along the animation, or makes 5 stops including the 0% and 100% marks (on the 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%) depends on which of the following step position is used:

Denotes a left-continuous function, so that the first jump happens when the animation begins.

Denotes a right-continuous function, so that the last jump happens when the animation ends. This is the default.

There is no jump on either end, effectively removing a step during the interpolation iteration. Instead, it holds at both the 0% mark and the 100% mark, each for 1/n of the duration.

Includes pauses at both the 0% and 100% marks, effectively adding a step during the animation iteration.

Same as  .

Same as  .

Equal to

Equal to

Note:

When you specify multiple comma-separated values on an   property, they are applied to the animations in the order in which the  s appear. For situations where the number of animations and   property values do not match, see  Setting multiple animation property values .

Note:

has the same effect when creating  CSS scroll-driven animations  as it does for regular time-based animations.

Description

Easing functions may be specified on individual keyframes in a   rule. If no   is specified on a keyframe, the corresponding value of   from the element to which the animation is applied is used for that keyframe.

Within a keyframe,   is an at-rule-specific descriptor, not the property of the same name. The timing is not being animated. Rather, a keyframe's easing function is applied on a property-by-property basis from the keyframe on which it is specified until the next keyframe specifying that property, or until the end of the animation if there is no subsequent keyframe specifying that property. As a result, an   specified on the   or   keyframe will never be used.

Formal definition

Initial value Applies to all elements,   and    pseudo-elements Inherited no Computed value as specified Animation type Not animatable

Formal syntax

Examples

All the examples in this section animate the   and   properties of several   elements with different   values. The width is being animated from   to  , and the background color is being animated from lime to magenta.

Linear function examples

The example demonstrates the effects of various   easing function values.

The following image shows graphs of all the   function values used in this example. Input progress (time) is plotted on the x-axis and output progress is plotted on the y-axis. As per the syntax, the input progress ranges from 0 to 100%, and the output ranges from 0 to 1.

Note that the output can go forward or backward.

Cubic-Bézier examples

The example demonstrates the effects of various bézier curve easing functions.

The following image shows graphs of all the cubic bézier function values used in this example. The input progress (time) ranges from 0 to 1 and the output progress ranges from 0 to 1.

Step examples

This example demonstrates the effects of several step easing function values.

The following image shows graphs of all the   function values used in this example. The input progress (time) and output progress range from 0 to 1.

title: appearance
content:
appearance

Baseline

Widely available

*

This feature is well established and works across many devices and browser versions. It’s been available across browsers since March 2022.

* Some parts of this feature may have varying levels of support.

Learn more

See full compatibility

Report feedback

The    CSS  property specifies the rendered appearance of replaced UI widget elements such as form controls. Most commonly, such elements are given native, platform-specific styling based on the operating system's theme, or a primitive appearance with styles that can be overridden using CSS.

Try it

Syntax

css

Values

The   property can be applied to all elements and pseudo-elements, but the effect of the specified value, if any, depends on the element to which it is applied.

Gives the widget a  primitive  appearance, making it stylable via CSS, while maintaining the widget's native functionality. This value does not affect non-widgets.

Sets interactive widgets to render with their  OS-native  appearance. Behaves as   on elements with no OS-native styling.

Relevant only to the   element and   pseudo-element, allowing them to be fully styled.

Has an effect similar to   on certain elements.

Causes the appearance of certain   types to  match the appearance of the   type .

When set on the   element, the drop-down picker's style  matches that of its default state .

Included for backwards compatibility; possible values include  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  ,  , and  . The values all behave as  : use   instead.

Note:

The specification also defines a   value. This is not yet supported by any browser.

Non-standard values

Some non-standard values are also supported in some browsers:

Makes the slider vertical when applied to   elements. To  create a vertical slider  you should instead set the   to   and the   to  .

Displays the Apple Pay logo when set on a  ,  , or   element of type   or  .

Description

The   property enables displaying elements in their OS-native style based on the operating system's theme, as well as the removal of any platform-native styling with the   value. Setting  , or otherwise changing the appearance of UI widgets, does not change the element's functionality.

While most elements in a document can be fully styled by CSS, UI controls ( widgets ) are typically rendered by the browser using the operating system's native UI styles. This  native  appearance differs between operating systems and browsers. In this default state, widgets have limited, if any, features that can be styled with CSS. Which elements have this native UI appearance is defined in HTML.

The   property provides some control over the appearance of HTML widgets that, by default, look like native operating system controls. Most notably, the   value suppresses some of a widget's native appearance. This results in a  primitive  look that can be styled via CSS while still maintaining functionality and supporting native user interactions.

Some widgets disappear completely when set to  . The hidden controls are still interactive, however. For example, clicking on a   associated with an   checkbox will toggle the checkbox's checked state.

Because   can cause a widget to be hidden, the   value is being added to provide widgets with a base appearance. When supported, the   value will ensure widgets maintain their native appearance while enabling CSS to be used to change a widget's styles that are not changeable by default. Unlike  , which can make radio buttons and checkboxes disappear,   gives the widget a primitive appearance with default native styles that are usable and interoperable, as well as enabling a good degree of customization via CSS. While this   value is not yet supported, the many   values provide similar functionality but are type-specific and not global.

Customizable select elements

The   value, which is relevant only to the   element and   pseudo-element, enables  styling   elements and the select picker  (which contains the   elements). The picker is rendered in the top layer, similar to a popover. When   is set, the picker can be positioned relative to the select (or other elements) using  CSS anchor positioning  features. In addition, the   value causes the   not to render outside the browser pane or to trigger built-in mobile operating system components. It is also no longer sized based on the width of the widest  .

See  Customizable select elements  for more information.

Prefixed non-standard values

Before standardization, the prefixed   and   properties allowed elements to be shown as widgets such as buttons or checkboxes. The following non-standard values may be encountered in legacy style sheets, most commonly as values of shadow DOM component  prefixed pseudo-elements .

Non-standard values

Authors are encouraged to use only standard keywords.

Formal definition

Initial value Applies to all elements Inherited no Computed value as specified Animation type discrete

Formal syntax

Examples

Basic example

This example demonstrates basic usage of the   property, altering the appearance of an   element in some browsers.

HTML

We include two   form controls along with their labels.

html

CSS

We set the element with the class of   to look like a text field.

css

Results

Depending on the browser, the spinner may be visually removed when the control is set to look like a text field. The   property has no effect on the functionality: for example, while there may no longer be a spinner to click on, the up and down cursor keys will still increment and decrement the value.

Appearance set to

The following example shows how to remove the default styling from a checkbox, a radio button, and a   element, and apply custom styling.

HTML

We include pairs of checkboxes, radio buttons, and   elements, along with their associated labels:

html

CSS

We apply styles to both   elements of type  ; these styles create a red square if the element is stylable. We set   on the   UI state for all inputs (  and  ), as well as to elements with the   class. This removes all the style of the radio button and checkbox, other than the margins, and allows any set styles to be applied. There are no alternative styles provided for the radio buttons or   elements for when   is set.

css

Result

Setting   enables UI elements to be styled, but it also runs the risk of hiding the widget. The unchecked checkbox, with its   defaulting to  , looks like a checkbox. Setting   in the   state enables it to be styled.

Like the unchecked checkbox, the unchecked radio button looks like the native UI widget, because it is. When in the checked state, with   applied, the radio button disappears; its functionality is retained, and only its margins affect page rendering.

Setting the appearance of a select

We can use the   property to opt-in to custom select functionality, enabling styling the   element and its picker, which represents the part of the form control that pops out of the page.

HTML

We include three   elements, with the same multiple   children. As with every  , we also include associated   elements. The third option has more text to demonstrate the effect of   on the width of the  :

html

CSS

We select the pickers of all the   elements using the   pseudo-element with the   parameter. We set the   value of all the pickers and one   element to  . We set the last   to  . The first   defaults to the   state:

css

css

We set values for the   and pickers'   and   properties to demonstrate the effects of the   values:

css

Results

While the   and   styles are defined on all the   elements and their pickers, the   styles only effect the picker where both the select and the picker have the   property set to  . The first and third selects look the same because   is a compatibility keyword.

Notice that, by default, the inline-size of the   is generally the inline-size of the   with the most text, and that the drop-down picker appears over the top of the rendered page when opened, making it not constrained by the surrounding page and therefore fully visible. These statements are no longer true when   is set.
